skill.name: wrike-automation
skill.description: Automates Wrike task, folder, project, blueprint, member, and reporting workflows through the Rube MCP Wrike toolkit.
skill.summary: Guide AI assistants through Wrike automation using Rube MCP tool workflows.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The static external command and weak cryptography findings are false positives caused by Markdown backticks and ordinary text, not executable code. The confirmed risks are operational: the skill depends on a third-party Rube MCP endpoint and documents Wrike actions that can permanently delete folders or spaces. Publish with a warning and require explicit confirmation for destructive actions.

medium_findings.title: Irreversible Wrike Deletion Operations
medium_findings.description: The skill documents DELETE_FOLDER and DELETE_SPACE workflows that can permanently remove Wrike folders, spaces, tasks, subfolders, and documents. This is a legitimate administrative capability, but it creates data-loss risk if an assistant executes it without clear user approval.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented tool names and warnings explicitly describe permanent deletion of Wrike resources. The risk is operational data loss, not malicious code execution.

low_findings.title: Third-Party MCP Endpoint Dependency
low_findings.description: The setup instructions require adding the Rube MCP endpoint. This is expected for the skill, but it means Wrike automation depends on an external service connection outside the local assistant runtime.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 20 contains a direct external MCP URL used for setup. The context is legitimate integration configuration, so the risk is limited to third-party network dependency.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Markdown References
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted inline backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell backtick execution. The file is Markdown guidance and the affected text names Rube and Wrike MCP tools or parameters, with no executable Ruby, shell script, or command substitution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Markdown list items, inline code spans, and fenced workflow examples. No evidence found of executable shell commands or user-controlled command construction.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Text Substring Matches
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts align with ordinary documentation words and Wrike field descriptions rather than cryptographic functions. No hashing, encryption, cipher selection, or security-sensitive crypto implementation appears in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines contain project-management documentation, such as descriptions and pagination notes. No evidence found of MD5, DES, SHA1, or other weak cryptographic algorithm usage.

low_findings.title: System Reconnaissance Findings Are Wrike Data Lookups
low_findings.description: The reconnaissance alerts point to documented Wrike lookup workflows, such as contacts, IDs, and blueprint parameters. These are normal Wrike automation steps and do not inspect the local system, network, or host environment.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines discuss Wrike object IDs, custom fields, and plan behavior. No evidence found of host reconnaissance commands or collection of local system details.

dangerous_patterns.title: Permanent Delete Tool Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes guidance for Wrike delete tools and correctly notes that folder and space deletion can be irreversible. Assistants should ask for explicit confirmation before using these operations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly names delete operations and describes permanent removal. The concern is confirmed, although it is an expected capability for Wrike administration.

content.value_statement: Wrike work often requires repeated task, folder, project, and assignment updates. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code structured workflows for using Wrike through Rube MCP.
actual_capabilities: Guides assistants to search current Rube MCP tool schemas before using Wrike tools.
Creates and modifies Wrike tasks with folders, assignees, status, importance, custom fields, and dates.
Manages Wrike folders and projects, including subfolder lookup and project filters.
Retrieves tasks, contacts, spaces, custom fields, and blueprint information for workflow planning.
Launches Wrike task blueprints with parent locations, rescheduling options, and entry limits.
Documents important Wrike pitfalls, including ID resolution, pagination, permissions, and destructive deletes.
limitations: Requires Rube MCP and an active Wrike OAuth connection before any workflow can run.
Wrike permissions, sharing rules, and subscription plan features can block some actions.
The skill provides workflow guidance and does not include custom code or validation scripts.
Destructive operations must be confirmed by the user because they can remove Wrike data.

use_cases.title: Create Structured Project Tasks
use_cases.description: Turn a work request into Wrike tasks with owners, dates, status, priority, and custom fields.

use_cases.title: Maintain Workspace Organization
use_cases.description: Create, update, and inspect folders or projects while respecting Wrike IDs and permissions.

use_cases.title: Launch Repeatable Workflows
use_cases.description: Use Wrike task blueprints to create standardized task sets for recurring business processes.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Task
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the Wrike automation skill to create a task in the correct folder. Search the current tool schema first, resolve the folder ID, and ask me for any missing title, owner, or due date.

prompt_templates.title: Update Project Work
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the Wrike project I name, find active tasks due this week, and prepare safe updates for status, assignees, and dates. Ask before making changes.

prompt_templates.title: Launch a Blueprint
prompt_templates.prompt: Find available Wrike task blueprints, help me choose the right one, and launch it in the target folder with rescheduling settings that match my start date.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Workspace Actions
prompt_templates.prompt: Inspect the requested Wrike space, list relevant folders, members, and high-risk actions, then propose a change plan. Do not delete or invite anyone without explicit confirmation.

output_examples.input: Create onboarding tasks for a new customer implementation in Wrike.
output: Resolved the target implementation folder before task creation.
Prepared onboarding tasks with owners, due dates, priority, and status.
Flagged missing custom field values that need user confirmation.

output_examples.input: Show active tasks due this week for a product launch project.
output: Listed active tasks from the selected launch project.
Grouped the work by owner and due date.
Highlighted tasks that need follow-up or reassignment.

output_examples.input: Launch our standard campaign blueprint for next month.
output: Found available campaign blueprints in Wrike.
Confirmed the parent folder and reschedule mode.
Prepared the launch request and noted that blueprint creation is asynchronous.

best_practices: Search current Rube MCP tool schemas before calling any Wrike tool.
Resolve folder, task, user, space, and custom field IDs from Wrike instead of guessing them.
Ask for explicit confirmation before deletes, invitations, sharing changes, or large updates.
anti_patterns: Do not use names or emails where a Wrike user ID is required.
Do not call DELETE_FOLDER or DELETE_SPACE without a clear user-approved target.
Do not assume custom statuses, custom fields, or admin actions are available on every Wrike plan.

faq.question: Does this skill connect directly to Wrike?
faq.answer: It uses Wrike through Rube MCP. A connected Wrike OAuth account is required before actions can run.

faq.question: Can it create Wrike tasks?
faq.answer: Yes. It guides task creation with folder IDs, titles, owners, dates, status, priority, and custom fields.

faq.question: Can it delete folders or spaces?
faq.answer: Yes, the documented Wrike tools include delete operations. These actions should require explicit user confirmation.

faq.question: Why must the assistant search tools first?
faq.answer: Rube MCP tool schemas can change. Searching first helps the assistant use current parameters.

faq.question: Does it handle custom fields?
faq.answer: Yes. It instructs the assistant to fetch custom field definitions before using custom field IDs and values.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with warnings. The main risks are third-party MCP use and destructive Wrike administration actions.
